journal-title-group.journal-title: Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise

title-group.article-title: Ice Slurry Mitigates Hyperventilation and Cerebral Hypoperfusion, and May Enhance Endurance Performance in the Heat

article-meta.aff: 1Institute of Health and Sport Sciences, University of Tsukuba, Tsukuba City, Ibaraki, JAPAN
2Advanced Research Initiative for Human High Performance (ARIHHP), University of Tsukuba, Tsukuba City, Ibaraki, JAPAN

author-notes.corresp: Address for correspondence: Naoto Fujii, Ph.D., Institute of Health and Sport Sciences, University of Tsukuba, Tsukuba City, Ibaraki 305-8574, Japan; E-mail: fujii.naoto.gb@u.tsukuba.ac.jp.

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Wolters Kluwer Health, Inc. on behalf of the American College of Sports Medicine.

license.license-p: This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-Non Commercial-No Derivatives License 4.0 (CCBY-NC-ND), where it is permissible to download and share the work provided it is properly cited. The work cannot be changed in any way or used commercially without permission from the journal.

sec.title: Purpose
sec.p: Hyperthermia causes hyperventilation and associated cerebral hypoperfusion, both of which may limit endurance performance in the heat. Here, we investigated the hypothesis that pre-exercise ice slurry ingestion may mitigate hyperventilation and cerebral hypoperfusion during exercise in the heat, enhancing endurance performance.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Twelve healthy men ingested 7.5 g·kg−1 body weight of ice slurry (Ice) or the same solution at 37°C (Con) within 30 min. Thereafter, the participants performed cycle exercise for 30 min at 55% of peak oxygen uptake in the heat (35°C), followed by a cycle exercise at 90% of peak oxygen uptake until exhaustion.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Esophageal, rectal, and gastrointestinal temperatures were lower throughout moderate-intensity exercise in the Ice than the Con trial (all P < 0.05). Correspondingly, minute ventilation was lower and cerebral blood flow index was higher in the Ice trial (both P ≤ 0.042, main effects of trial). Subjective severity of diarrhea and stomachache was increased in the Ice versus Con trial (both P ≤ 0.041, main effects of trial). The duration of high-intensity exercise was not statistically different between the two trials (P = 0.160), although 8 out of the 10 participants exercised for a longer duration in the Ice trial, with a moderate effect size (r = 0.47). Based on z-scores and Grubbs’ test, one outlier, who exhibited an extreme increase in ventilation and gastrointestinal complaints following ice slurry ingestion, showed a marked reduction in exercise duration.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Pre-exercise ice slurry ingestion alleviates hyperventilation and cerebral hypoperfusion during submaximal exercise in the heat and potentially improves subsequent high-intensity endurance performance, although the adverse effects associated with ice slurry ingestion might hinder the ergogenic effect in some individuals.

body.p: Pre-exercise ingestion of ice slurry, a sherbet-like beverage, is known as a precooling strategy (1–3). This cooling effect contributes to an increase in heat storage capacity and a reduction in perceptual stress (e.g., perceived exertion and thermal sensation/comfort), which have the potential to improve endurance exercise performance in the heat (1,2,4,5). However, this ergogenic effect is not always detected (6), possibly due to gastrointestinal issues (7). Nevertheless, how ice slurry ingestion modulates physiological strain experienced during exercise in the heat remain understood. This information is necessary to determine whether pre-exercise ice slurry ingestion is indeed effective for improving exercise performance during heat stress.

During prolonged moderate-intensity exercise in the heat, elevations in core temperature (hyperthermia) lead to increases in minute ventilation ( V˙E) (8–10). Specifically, when V˙E is expressed as a function of esophageal temperature (an index of core temperature), V˙E increases linearly with rising core temperature. The slope of this relationship appears to reflect the sensitivity of hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation (11–13). This hyperventilation increases the work of breathing (energy demand) (14), possibly compromising exercise efficiency. Although the mechanisms underlying hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation in humans remain unclear, carotid bodies might be involved, although results are inconclusive (15,16). Animal studies suggested that elevated temperature in the central nerves (medulla oblongata, hypothalamus, and spinal cord), carotid bodies, and muscle afferents may increase ventilation (17–20). Moreover, an animal study demonstrated that intra-abdominal warming increases ventilation (21,22), suggesting that abdominal temperature may also play a role in the control of breathing. In this context, decreases in gastrointestinal temperature achieved by pre-exercise ice slurry ingestion may reduce the sensitivity of hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation during prolonged exercise in the heat in humans, although this possibility has not yet been demonstrated. In addition, reductions in core temperature mediated by ice slurry ingestion may correspondingly reduce V˙E during exercise in the heat, independently of the influence of gastrointestinal temperature on the sensitivity of hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation. However, whether pre-exercise ice slurry ingestion attenuates ventilation during prolonged moderate-intensity exercise in the heat remains unclear.

In exercising humans, cerebral blood flow is regulated by several factors, including arterial blood CO2 partial pressure, arterial blood pressure, hydration status, and regional increases in cerebral neural activity and metabolism (23–25). Hyperthermia can also modulate cerebral blood flow such that it leads to a progressive reduction in middle cerebral artery mean blood velocity (MCAVmean; an index of cerebral blood flow) during prolonged moderate-intensity exercise in the heat (9,26). This cerebral hypoperfusion may accelerate increases in brain temperature (27,28), potentially limiting endurance performance in the heat via central mechanisms. Notably, the primary factor of cerebral hypoperfusion during exercise in the heat is a reduction in arterial blood CO2 partial pressure attributed to hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation (14,29,30). Accordingly, mitigating hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation associated with ice slurry ingestion, if it occurs, may also alleviate cerebral hypoperfusion during exercise in the heat, potentially improving endurance performance.

In the present study, we investigated the hypothesis that pre-exercise ice slurry ingestion would mitigate hyperventilation during exercise in the heat by lowering core temperature and the sensitivity of hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation through reductions in gastrointestinal temperature. We also hypothesized that pre-exercise ice slurry ingestion would alleviate cerebral hypoperfusion and improve endurance performance in the heat.

sec.title: Ethical Approval
sec.p: This study was approved by the Human Subjects Committee of the University of Tsukuba (No. 020-181), in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki, except for registration in a clinical database. Written informed consent was obtained from all participants.

sec.title: Participants
sec.p: Twelve healthy young males (age: 22 ± 3 yr, height: 1.70 ± 0.07 m, weight: 60.9 ± 7.4 kg, peak oxygen uptake ( V˙O2peak): 51.8 ± 9.3 mL·kg−1·min−1) volunteered to participate in this study. Based on data from a previous study, we determined that a minimum sample size of 7 or 8 was needed to detect a significant difference in the sensitivity of hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation during exercise in the heat (31), or prolonged exercise duration with ice slurry ingestion (1) compared with control trials (calculated effect size, d = 1.29 and 1.21, respectively), with 80% power and a significance level of 0.05. None of the participants were smokers and reported a history of specific medical conditions or taking prescription medication. Before each session, participants were instructed to abstain from consuming alcohol, caffeine, and over-the-counter medications, and performing strenuous exercise for at least 24 h.

sec.title: Preliminary Session
sec.p: Before the experimental session, all participants completed an incremental cycling test on a customized semirecumbent cycle ergometer (818E; Monark, Stockholm, Sweden) to assess their V˙O2peak. The V˙O2peak test was conducted in an environmental chamber (Fuji Medical Science Co., Ltd, Chiba, Japan) maintained at 20°C and 50% relative humidity. During the test, the expired volume and gases were analyzed continuously using a metabolic cart (AE310s; Minato Medical Science, Osaka, Japan). The flow sensor was calibrated using an appurtenant calibration syringe capable of blowing a fixed volume of air (2.0 L). The O2 and CO2 sensors were calibrated with reference gases of known concentration (O2 15.10%, CO2 5.01%, N2 balance). The V˙O2peak test protocol began with a light warm-up (30 W, 60 rpm) for 3 min, followed by an incremental exercise. The exercise began at a workload of 90 W, and the load increased by 30 W every 1 min until 150 W, after which the load increased by 15 W every 1 min until exhaustion. Participants maintained pedaling rate of 60 rpm, and volitional exhaustion was defined as an inability to sustain a pedaling rate of more than 55 rpm. V˙O2peak was determined as the highest O2 uptake ( V˙O2) recorded over a 30-s period.

sec.title: Experimental Design
sec.p: The experimental procedure is illustrated in Figure 1. The experimental session was conducted a minimum of 2 d after completion of the preliminary session. On the night before the experiment, the participants drank 550 mL of mineral water. In addition, participants consumed a light meal and 550 mL of mineral water 2 h before the experiment, after which they refrained from consuming any food. Upon arrival at the laboratory at 0900 h, hydration status was confirmed by assessing urine-specific gravity of <1.025 (32) using a digital urine-specific gravity scale (T3-SE; Atago, Tokyo, Japan). If the urine-specific gravity was ≥1.025, an additional 550 mL of mineral water was consumed. We then obtained their body mass (with shorts only) using a weight scale platform (model ICS429; Mettler Toledo, Schwerzenbach, Switzerland). Thereafter, participants self-inserted a probe for measuring esophageal temperature (Tes) and rectal temperature. After a heart rate (HR) monitor and electrodes for measuring the electrocardiogram were attached, the participants entered an environmental chamber regulated to 35°C and 50% relative humidity wherein they spent ~60 min resting on a semirecumbent cycle ergometer. During this time, instrumentation was applied. Before 5-min baseline measurement, the participants ingested a pill (e-Celsius; BodyCap, Caen, France) for measuring gastrointestinal temperature with 50 mL of mineral water. We believe that the pill ingested immediately before the baseline would more reflect stomach temperature throughout the experiment, as the pill likely remained in the stomach for up to ~200 min (33). Baseline measurements were collected for 10 min while the participants remained sitting, after which the participants ingested 7.5 g·kg−1 body weight of ice slurry (Ice trial) (Pocari sweat ice slurry; Otsuka Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd, Japan) or the same heated to ~37°C (Con trial) within 30 min. The beverage contained 27.3 g of carbohydrates, 43.2 mg of sodium, and 18 mg of potassium per 100 g. To standardize the ingestion rate, participants were administered five equal portions of the beverage (i.e., 1.5 g·kg−1) at 5-min intervals. Five minutes following the final beverage ingestion, another 10-min resting measurements (post-ingestion) were collected. The participants then performed the cycle exercise at 55% of V˙O2peak for 30 min in the heat (ambient temperature = 35°C, relative humidity = 50%, and wind speed <0.2 m·s−1) followed by exercise at 90% of V˙O2peak until exhaustion (performance test). This protocol was adopted in anticipation of actual competitions where high performance needs to be achieved during the final spurt at the end of a competition under heat-stressed conditions. Fixed exercise intensities at 55% and 90% of V˙O2peak allowed the assessment of physiological and perceptual changes associated with ice slurry ingestion, excluding the influence of varying exercise intensity, which is not possible with self-paced exercise. Following cessation of the exercise, measurement site of skin blood flow was heated locally to obtain maximal skin blood flow (details are described hereinafter). Post-exercise and post-voiding body weights were then recorded.

caption.p: A schematic overview of the experimental protocol. Participants ingested 7.5 g·kg−1 body weight of a thermoneutral control drink or ice slurry within 30 min. After the ingestion, the participants engaged in a 30-min exercise on a semirecumbent cycle ergometer, maintaining an intensity equivalent to 55% of peak oxygen uptake in the heat (35°C and 50% relative humidity). Subsequently, participants exercised at 90% of peak oxygen uptake until exhaustion. FeNO, fractional nitric oxide concentration in exhaled breath.

sec.title: Body temperatures
sec.p: Tes and skin temperature were measured using copper-constantan thermocouples (made in-house). The probe for measuring Tes was inserted through the nasal passage to a distance equivalent to one-fourth of the participant’s height, with its location in the esophagus estimated to be posterior to the lower border of the left atrium (34). Because Tes can reflect arterial blood temperature, in the present study, Tes was deemed as an index of core temperature. Mean skin temperature was calculated from seven skin temperatures weighted to the following regional proportions: 7% forehead, 14% forearm, 5% hand, 7% foot, 13% lower leg, 19% thigh, and 35% chest (35). Tes and skin temperature were recorded continuously and stored in a computer (Versa Pro; NEC, Tokyo, Japan) through a data logger system (GM10; Yokogawa, Tokyo, Japan). The rectal temperature probe was inserted to a depth of 15 cm past the anal sphincter (401; NikkisoTherm Co., Ltd, Tokyo, Japan). In addition to Tes, we measured rectal temperature as another core temperature index, as Tes is possibly influenced by ice slurry ingestion, whereas rectal temperature is not. Rectal temperature data were also stored in the computer (Versa Pro; NEC) through a data logger system (N543; NikkisoTherm Co., Ltd). Gastrointestinal temperature data were sampled every 30 s and stored within the pill and downloaded to receivers (e-Viewer; BodyCap).

sec.title: Respiratory variables
sec.p: Inspired and expired gases were analyzed using a metabolic cart (AE310s; Minato Medical Science). The calibration procedures for the measuring respiratory gases were identical to those outlined in the “Preliminary Session.” Continuous breath-to-breath measurements of V˙E and tidal volume (VT) (both adjusted to BTPS), respiratory frequency (fR), V˙O2, CO2 output ( V˙CO2), end-tidal CO2 partial pressure (PETCO2), ventilatory equivalent for V˙O2 ( V˙E/ V˙O2), ventilatory equivalent for V˙CO2, ( V˙E/ V˙CO2), and the respiratory exchange ratio were collected and stored in a computer (Satellite Pro; Toshiba, Tokyo, Japan).

sec.title: Cardiovascular variables
sec.p: HR was recorded every 5 s using an HR monitor (RS400; Polar Electro, Kempele, Finland). Arterial systolic and diastolic pressures were measured from the right upper right arm every 1 min using an automated sphygmomanometer (Tango M2; SunTech Medical, Durham, NC). Mean arterial pressure was calculated as the diastolic pressure plus one-third of the pulse pressure. MCAVmean, an index of cerebral blood flow, was determined using a transcranial Doppler device (Doppler-Box™X; DWL, Lindau, Germany). A 2-MHz Doppler probe was affixed with an adjustable headband to the left temporal region and insonated the area of middle cerebral artery at a depth and width of 45–60 and 8 mm, respectively. Before the first trial, the position and probe angle that provided the optimal Doppler signal were determined and photographed so they could be used for the second trial. To account for the changes in perfusion pressure on cerebral blood flow, middle cerebral artery vascular conductance index (MCACVCi) was calculated as MCAVmean divided by the mean arterial pressure. An index of skin blood flow data was obtained at two locations on the left forearm and left chest using a laser-Doppler flowmeter, comprising a central laser irradiation module surrounded by four laser-receiving components (Ø 4.0 mm, ALF21D; Advance, Tokyo, Japan). The laser-Doppler probe was ensconced within the Peltier unit (ADT1045; Advance), which can control locak skin temperature. Local skin temperature was elevated to 43 °C by the Peltier unit after the exercise and this heating was sustained for 20–40 min, until a plateau in skin blood flow was visually confirmed. Except for the last stage of the experiment, local skin temperature was not controlled throughout the experiment. Cutaneous vascular conductance was calculated as the ratio of cutaneous red blood cell flux to mean arterial pressure. All cardiovascular variables except HR data were continuously recorded and stored in a computer via a data acquisition system (Powerlab 8/35; ADInstruments, Dunedin, New Zealand).

sec.title: Sweat rate
sec.p: Sweat rate (on the left forearm and left chest) was measured using the ventilated-capsule method. Dry N2 gas was supplied to the capsule (2.83 cm2) at a rate of 1.0 L·min−1. The humidity of the N2 gas flowing out of the capsule was measured using a capacitance hygrometer (HMT333; Vaisala, Helsinki, Finland). Sweat rate data were recorded continuously and stored in a computer (Versa Pro; NEC) through a data logger system (GM10; Yokogawa).

sec.title: Perceptions
sec.p: During the experiment, ratings of perceived exertion (Borg’s scale) (36), effort of breathing (10 scale: 1, very easy; 3, moderate; 5, hard; 7 very hard) (37), thermal sensation (40 scale: −20, unbearably cold; 0, neutral; 20, unbearably hot) (38), and thermal comfort (9 scale: 5, uncomfortable; 7, very uncomfortable; 9, unbearably uncomfortable) (38) were measured every 5 min. Using a visual analog scale on a 100-mm horizontal straight line, corresponding to a score from 0 to 100, we assessed gastrointestinal complaints: feeling of diarrhea (0 = none, 100 = most severe), stomachache (0 = none, 100 = very painful), and bloating (0 = none, 100 = very full). Gastrointestinal complaints were assessed at pre- and post-ingestion, 15 and 30 min during exercise, and immediately after high-intensity exercise. In addition, next morning the participants answered subjective severity of diarrhea from the end of the experiment until bedtime, using a visual analog scale (0–100 scale: 0 = none, 100 = most severe).

sec.title: Plasma volume and osmolality
sec.p: All blood samples taken from fingertip were separated into aliquots to measure the hemoglobin concentration, hematocrit, and plasma osmolality. Hemoglobin concentration was measured using the azidemethemoglobin method (B-Hemoglobin; Hemocue, Brea, CA, USA), whereas hematocrit was measured using the microcentrifuge method. Hemoglobin concentrations and hematocrits were used to estimate the percent change in plasma volume, which was calculated using the equation created by Dill and Costill (39). Blood samples used for the measurement of plasma osmolality were transferred to microtubes and centrifuged at 4°C for 5 min, after which the osmolality of the separated plasma was immediately assessed using the freezing point depression method (Micro-Osmometer 210; Fiske, Norwood, MA). Each blood variable was assessed three times, and the mean value was utilized for data analysis.

sec.title: Fractional nitric oxide concentration in exhaled breath
sec.p: Fractional nitric oxide concentration in exhaled breath (FeNO), a noninvasive surrogate marker of airway inflammation, may be influenced by pre-exercise ice slurry ingestion, as airway cooling during exercise may reduce FeNO via bronchoconstriction (40). Consequently, we measured FeNO using portable devices (NO breath; Bedfont Scientific Ltd., Harrietsham, UK). Participants exhaled slowly for 12 s with an exhalation pressure of 5–20 cm H2O to maintain a fixed flow rate of 50 mL·s−1 through the mouthpiece connected to the device. Two measurements of FeNO were taken before baseline and during local heating (i.e., post-exercise). The mean of these two values was used for data analysis.

sec.title: Data Analysis
sec.p: Physiological data were averaged every 5 min during the submaximal exercise and used for time-dependent data analysis. The same variables during exercise were obtained as values averaged at given Tes levels (37.0°C, 37.4°C, 37.8°C, and 38.2°C) and were utilized for Tes-dependent analysis. For resting data, we used the averaged data from the last 5 min of the 10-min baseline and post-ingestion period, respectively. The data for MCAVmean (MCAVmean%baseline) and MCACVCi (MCACVCi%baseline) were calculated as relative values compared with baseline levels, which were assigned a value of 100%. Cutaneous vascular conductance was normalized to the maximum conductance, with values expressed as a percentage of the maximum (%max). The values during the high-intensity exercise were assessed by averaging data every 30 s. Furthermore, the value during the final 30 s in one trial, in which participants exercised for a shorter duration, was assessed and compared with the value at the corresponding time point in the other trial (i.e., Iso-time: 122 ± 70 s). The 30-s highest or lowest value achieved during the high-intensity exercise was defined as peak or nadir value, respectively. Due to technical issues, the measurement of respiratory variables, skin blood flow on the forearm, and sweat rate on the forearm was unsuccessful in one, two, and one participants, respectively. Two participants did not perform high-intensity exercise because they exceeded Tes 39.0°C, a criterion for exercise cessation due to ethical considerations, at the end of moderate-intensity exercise.

We plotted V˙E as a function of Tes and conducted simple linear regression analyses using GraphPad Prism (v. 9.5.1; GraphPad Software, La Jolla, CA, USA). The slope of the regression lines relating V˙E to Tes assessed by a least-squares fit crossed was considered to define to sensitivity of hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation. For the analyses, 30-s averaged data were used. In addition, to exclude the fast component of V˙E kinetics, only respiratory data collected after the first 5 min of the exercise were analyzed.

The change (Δ) from the Ice to the Con trial was assessed for V˙E, PETCO2, MCAVmean%baseline, and gastrointestinal complaints measured at the end of moderate-intensity exercise, and duration of high-intensity exercise. Δ values were used for outlier analyses (see the Statistical Analysis section).

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: The normal distribution of data used for time- and Tes-dependent analyses was checked using the Shapiro–Wilk test. For variables that did not pass the Shapiro–Wilk test, we confirmed a minor deviation from normality by visual inspection with Q–Q plots. Physiological variables and perceptions collected at post-ingestion (time 0) and during exercise were analyzed using two-factor repeated-measures ANOVA with the factors of trial (Con and Ice) and exercise time (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 min into submaximal exercise). Physiological variables collected during high-intensity exercise were also analyzed using two-factor repeated-measures ANOVA with the factors of trial (Con and Ice) and time (Iso-time and peak or nadir). For time-dependent changes in fingertip blood variables, we also used two-factor repeated-measures ANOVA with factors of trial (Con and Ice) and time (baseline, post-ingestion, end of submaximal exercise, and post-performance test). The Tes-dependent data, with trial (Con and Ice) and Tes level (37.0°C, 37.4°C, 37.8°C, and 38.2°C) as factors, were analyzed by fitting a mixed model to accommodate missing value that were present at some Tes levels. Below Tes of 37.0°C and above Tes of 38.2°C, the number of participants was even smaller. We therefore decided to analyze data at Tes levels wherein sufficient number of participants remained. When an interaction was detected, post hoc multiple comparisons were performed with the Bonferroni correction. If a main effect of trial was detected without interaction, or a difference in trial throughout submaximal exercise was detected by post hoc multiple comparisons, pooled data throughout the submaximal exercise within each trial were calculated. Paired t-tests were used for between-trial comparisons of the pooled data, each variable at baseline, the slope of regression lines plotting V˙E against Tes, gastrointestinal complaints following high-intensity exercise, and body weight losses. Because exercise duration, the pooled feeling of diarrhea and stomachache throughout the submaximal exercise, and the subjective severity of diarrhea post-experiment were not normally distributed, the Wilcoxon test was used to assess differences between trials. Effect size (r; 0.10 = small, 0.30 = medium, 0.50 = large) for parametric pairwise comparisons was calculated using Student’s t-distribution (t) and degrees of freedom (df), whereas effect size for nonparametric pairwise comparison was calculated from the test statistic (Z) and sample size (41) using SPSS (v29.0; IBM SPSS Statistic, Armonk, NY). To detect outlier in the Δvalues, z-scores were calculated to confirm if the absolute z-score (|z|) exceeded ±2.0, a threshold generally considered indicative of a potential outlier (42). Upon detection, the Grubbs test, another statistical method for identifying outliers, was also employed to double-check outliners. Normally distributed variables were presented as mean ± SD, whereas non-normally distributed variables were reported as median and range. Values of P < 0.05 were considered significant. Statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism (v 9.5.1; GraphPad Software), except for the manual calculations of effect sizes for parametric and nonparametric pairwise comparisons, as well as z-scores assessed by SPSS.

sec.title: Baseline Resting Data
sec.p: Baseline resting physiological variables and perceptions did not differ between the two trials (all P ≥ 0.066; Supplemental Table 1, Supplemental Digital Content, http://links.lww.com/MSS/D197).

sec.title: Body temperature and ventilatory responses
sec.p: Tes and rectal and gastrointestinal temperatures gradually increased with exercise duration, but they were lower in the Ice than the Con trial from pre-exercise resting state to the end of submaximal exercise in the heat (Figs. 2A–C, all P ≤ 0.043), with high effect sizes (Table 1). Mean skin temperature and V˙E also gradually increased in both trials, with the Ice being lower than the Con trial throughout, as evidenced by a main effect of trial (Figs. 2D, E), with high effect sizes (Table 1). Changes in PETCO2, VT, fR, V˙O2, V˙E/ V˙O2, and V˙E/ V˙CO2 were similar in the two trials (Table 2 and Fig. 2F), but V˙CO2 and respiratory exchange ratio were lower in the Ice than the Con trial, as evidenced by main effect of trial (Table 2), with high effect sizes (Table 1).

caption.p: Time-dependent changes in esophageal temperature (A), rectal temperature (B), gastrointestinal temperature (C), mean skin temperature (D), minute ventilation (E; n = 11), and end-tidal CO2 partial pressure (F; n = 11) before and during moderate-intensity exercise in each trial. Data were analyzed by two-factor repeated-measures ANOVA. Bold denotes statistically significant values. When an interaction was detected, post hoc multiple comparisons were performed with the Bonferroni correction. *P < 0.05 vs Con. Symbols are means ± SD; n = 12 unless otherwise indicated.
Time-dependent changes in physiological variables and perceptions before and during moderate-intensity exercise.

tr.td: Ventilatory equivalent for oxygen uptake, units (n = 11)
Ventilatory equivalent for carbon dioxide output, units (n = 11)
Middle cerebral artery mean blood velocity, cm·s−1
Middle cerebral artery vascular conductance index, units

fn.p: Values are means ± SD; n = 12 unless otherwise indicated. Con indicates control solution (37°C) trial whereas Ice indicates ice slurry ingestion trial. Data were analyzed by two-factor repeated-measures ANOVA, except for middle cerebral artery vascular conductance index and perceptions, which were analyzed using the mixed effect model. Bold denotes statistically significant values. When an interaction was detected, post hoc multiple comparisons were performed with the Bonferroni correction.

caption.p: Statistical outcomes of physiological variables and perceptions from post-ingestion to end of moderate-intensity exercise.

tr.td: Middle cerebral artery mean blood velocity, %baseline

fn.p: Data are derived by averaging data from post-ingestion to end of submaximal exercise when a main effect of trial for two-way ANOVA was detected without interaction, or a difference in trial from post-ingestion to end of submaximal exercise was detected by post hoc multiple comparisons. Con indicates control solution (37°C) trial whereas Ice indicates ice slurry ingestion trial. Between-trial differences (Δ) with 95% confidence intervals (CI) are reported. The P values derived from a paired t-test or the Wilcoxon test, and effect size (r) calculated, both for comparing the two trials, are provided.

sec.p: Figure 3A shows the changes in V˙E plotted as function of Tes. The averaged slope of the regression line relating V˙E to Tes did not differ between the Con and Ice trials (8.8 ± 3.4 vs 7.2 ± 3.4 L·min−1·°C−1, P = 0.175) with a moderate effect size of r = 0.40 (Fig. 3B). At corresponding Tes (range, 37.0°C–38.2°C), gastrointestinal temperature was lower in the Ice than Con trial (Fig. 3C), but there were no between-trial differences in V˙E (Fig. 3D).

caption.p: Minute ventilation data plotted against esophageal temperature during moderate-intensity exercise (A). Con indicates control solution (37°C) trial whereas Ice indicates ice slurry ingestion trial. Symbols represent the mean values ± SD. Also presented are averaged the slope of the relation between the two variables (B) (n = 11). A paired t-test was used for the sensitivity. Shown are mean with individual data. C and D show esophageal temperature-dependent changes in gastrointestinal temperature and minute ventilation, respectively (n = 11), during moderate-intensity exercise in each trial. The data were analyzed by fitting a mixed-effects model. Bold denotes statistically significant values. When an interaction was detected, post hoc multiple comparisons were performed with the Bonferroni correction. Symbols represent the mean values ± SD. The numbers by the symbols indicate the number of subjects remaining at the corresponding temperature.

sec.title: Cardiovascular and cerebrovascular responses
sec.p: There were no-between trial differences in HR, MCAVmean, and MCACVCi during submaximal exercise (Table 2). Mean arterial pressure was higher in the Ice than the Con trial during the first 5 min of the exercise, but otherwise, there was no difference between the two trials (Fig. 4A). MCAVmean%baseline was higher in the Ice than the Con trial throughout the submaximal exercise, as evidenced by main effect of trial (Fig. 4B), with a high effect size (Table 1).

caption.p: Time-dependent changes in mean arterial pressure (A), middle cerebral artery mean blood velocity (B), thermal sensation (C), thermal comfort (D), feeling of diarrhea (E), and stomachache (F) before and during moderate-intensity exercise in each trial. Con indicates control solution (37°C) trial whereas Ice indicates ice slurry ingestion trial. Data were analyzed by two-factor repeated-measures ANOVA. Bold denotes statistically significant values. When an interaction was detected, post hoc multiple comparisons were performed with the Bonferroni correction. *P < 0.05 vs Con. Symbols are means ± SD; n = 12.

sec.title: Thermoregulatory responses
sec.p: Cutaneous vascular conductance was lower in the Ice than the Con trial on the forearm after the ingestion, whereas it was lower in the Ice than the Con trial from 5 to 15 min of the exercise regardless of the skin site (forearm and chest) (Supplemental Table 2, Supplemental Digital Content, all P ≤ 0.003, http://links.lww.com/MSS/D197). Sweat rate on the chest was lower in the Ice than the Con trial (P = 0.028, main effect of trial), with a high effect size of r = 0.60, but forearm sweat rate did not differ between trials (Supplemental Table 2, Supplemental Digital Content, http://links.lww.com/MSS/D197). At corresponding Tes levels, however, there were no between-trial differences in cutaneous vascular conductance and sweat rate regardless of the skin site (Supplemental Fig. 1, Supplemental Digital Content, http://links.lww.com/MSS/D197).

sec.title: Perceptions
sec.p: Thermal sensation scores were lower in the Ice than the Con trial after the ingestion (Fig. 4C, P < 0.001). Thermal comfort scores were lower in the Ice than the Con trial after the ingestion and at 5 min of exercise (both P < 0.001 for a main effect of trial), with a high effect size (Fig. 4D, Table 1). Increases in ratings of perceived exertion and effort of breathing were similar in the two trials. The reported feeling of diarrhea and stomachache scores were higher in the Ice than the Con trial, as evidenced by a main effect of trial (Figs. 4E, F), with high effect sizes (Table 1). Feeling of fullness scores did not differ between trials. The subjective severity of diarrhea scores post-experiment did not differ between trials (Con vs Ice, 0 (0, 11) vs 0 (0, 23) units; P > 0.999).

sec.title: High-Intensity Exercise
sec.p: Ten of the 12 participants completed high-intensity exercise following the moderate-intensity exercise. The duration of high-intensity exercise did not differ between the two trials (Con vs Ice, 107 (50, 243) vs 180 (63, 254) s; P = 0.160), but 8 of the 10 participants exercised for longer duration in the Ice with a moderate effect size of r = 0.47 (Fig. 5A). Gastrointestinal temperature was lower in the Ice than the Con trial (Table 3, P = 0.025, main effect of trial). However, there were no between-trial differences in Tes, rectal and mean skin temperature (Table 3). There were no between-trial differences in respiratory, cardiovascular, and thermoregulatory variables, except for higher V˙O2 (Table 3) and lower cutaneous vascular conductance and sweat rate on the forearm (Supplemental Table 3, Supplemental Digital Content, http://links.lww.com/MSS/D197) in the Ice than the Con trial. Following high-intensity exercise, reported scores for diarrhea (Con vs. Ice: 7.3 ± 10.9 vs. 10.6 ± 15.7, P = 0.279), stomachache (8.5 ± 10.8 vs. 6.8 ± 8.1, P = 0.508), and fullness (13.3 ± 16.9 vs. 12.1 ± 12.4, P = 0.802) did not differ between trials.

caption.p: Time to exhaustion during high-intensity exercise following moderate-intensity exercise in each trial (n = 10). Con indicates control solution (37°C) trial whereas Ice indicates ice slurry ingestion trial. The Wilcoxon test was used to assess differences between trials. Individual data and means are presented.

tr.td: Ventilatory equivalent for oxygen uptake, units
Ventilatory equivalent for carbon dioxide output, units
Middle cerebral artery mean blood velocity, cm·s−1 (n = 5)
Middle cerebral artery vascular conductance index, units (n = 5)

fn.p: Values are means ± SD; n = 10 unless otherwise indicated. Con indicates control solution (37°C) trial whereas Ice indicates ice slurry ingestion trial. The value during the final 30 s in one trial, in which participants exercised for a shorter duration, was assessed and compared with the value at the corresponding time point in the other trial (i.e., Iso-time: 122 ± 70 s). Moreover, the highest or lowest value achieved during performance test for 30 s was defined as peak or nadir value, respectively. Data were analyzed by two-factor repeated-measures ANOVA. Bold denotes statistically significant values. When an interaction was detected, post hoc multiple comparisons were performed with the Bonferroni correction.

sec.title: Outlier Analyses
sec.p: In one participant, the z-scores for Δ values for V˙E, PETCO2, MCAVmean%baseline, feeling of diarrhea, stomachache, and exercise duration were 2.56, −2.89, −1.48, 3.05, 2.90, and −2.37, respectively. In this participant, Δ values for V˙E, PETCO2, feeling of diarrhea, stomachache, and exercise duration were identified as outliers based of the Grubbs test. Exercise duration was significantly longer in the Ice trial compared with the Con trial after excluding the participant identified as an outlier (167 ± 68 vs 126 ± 77 s, P = 0.019, r = 0.72) (Supplemental Fig. 2, Supplemental Digital Content, http://links.lww.com/MSS/D167).

sec.title: Other Variables
sec.p: Plasma volume decreased throughout the experiment, whereas plasma osmolality elevated, with comparable magnitudes observed between the two trials (Supplemental Fig. 3, Supplemental Digital Content, http://links.lww.com/MSS/D167). FeNO (time: P = 0.359, trial: P = 0.859, interaction: P = 0.743; n = 11) remained consistent between pre- and post-experiment in both trials and did not differ between the Con and Ice trials (pre-experiment: 23.4 ± 16.4 vs 23.9 ± 16.1 ppb, post-experiment: 22.5 ± 13.2 vs 22.5 ± 13.4 ppb). Body weight losses through the experiment were similar between the Con and Ice trials (0.62 ± 0.22 vs 0.61 ± 0.23 kg body weight loss, P = 0.914).

sec.title: Ventilatory and cardiovascular responses
sec.p: We observed that pre-exercise ice slurry ingestion decreased Tes and rectal and gastrointestinal temperatures before exercise compared with the Con trial, and these temperatures remained lower throughout the moderate-intensity exercise (Figs. 2A–C). Correspondingly, as per our hypothesis, the reductions in V˙E associated with ice slurry ingestion were observed throughout the moderate-intensity exercise (Fig. 2E). Similarly, using a pre- (13) or inter-exercise (43) cold water immersion, we previously reported that subsequent V˙E during exercise in the heat decreased in parallel with reductions in core temperature. Based on the combined findings of our present and previous studies, cooling strategies that accompanies reductions in core temperature, whether applied externally or internally, may help alleviate respiratory strain during exercise in the heat.

Regarding the potential influence of gastrointestinal temperature on the reduced V˙E, the sensitivity of hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation was slightly lower (−1.6 L·min−1·°C−1) in the Ice than the Con trial, with a moderate effect size (r = 0.42), although the difference was not statistically significant, contrary to our hypothesis (Fig. 3B). The reduced sensitivity associated with ice slurry ingestion only partially (~34%) accounts for the observed reductions in V˙E. In line with this, V˙E was similar between the two trials at comparable Tes levels (Fig. 3D) despite a maximum difference in gastrointestinal temperature of ~1.2°C (Fig. 3C). Therefore, the reduction in gastrointestinal temperature associated with ice slurry ingestion is not a major factor underlying the reduced V˙E. A greater reduction in gastrointestinal temperature may be necessary to affect the ventilatory response, or temperature sensors in the gastrointestinal organs may have minimal influence on ventilatory response in humans. Taken together, our results suggest that during submaximal exercise in the heat, pre-exercise ice slurry ingestion mitigates exercise ventilation primarily by lowering core temperature rather than reducing the sensitivity of hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation through reductions in gastrointestinal temperature.

Consistent with previous reports (9,26), MCAVmean (cm·s−1) declined as exercise continued in both trials (Table 2). Importantly, as per our hypothesis, pre-exercise ice slurry ingestion increased MCAVmean%baseline (i.e., expressed relative to baseline) throughout moderate-intensity exercise in comparison to the Con trial, as evidenced by the main effect of trial (Fig. 4B). The higher MCAVmean%baseline may have enhanced cerebral heat removal (44), thereby reducing brain temperature, contributing to the observed reductions in V˙E during moderate-intensity exercise, as elevated brain temperature can stimulate ventilation in animal studies (17,20). In addition, the higher MCAVmean%baseline may have improved CO2 washout within the brain (45), attenuating the activity of central chemoreceptors and consequently reducing V˙E during the exercise. The extent to which these mechanisms, alongside the reduction in core temperature mentioned previously, contributes to the observed decrease in V˙E ​during exercise warrants further investigation.

In the present study, end-tidal CO2 partial pressure (Fig. 2F), plasma volume, plasma osmolality (Supplemental Fig. 3, Supplemental Digital Content, http://links.lww.com/MSS/D167), and body weight losses were similar between the two trials, suggesting that factor(s) other than arterial CO2 partial pressure (14,29,30) and hydration status (24) mediate(s) the elevated MCAVmean%baseline. Given that mean arterial pressure was elevated during the early stage of moderate-intensity exercise in the Ice compared with the Con trial (Fig. 4A), presumably in part due to attenuated cutaneous vasodilation associated with lower thermal drive (Supplemental Table 2, Supplemental Digital Content, http://links.lww.com/MSS/D167), perfusion pressure would be higher in the Ice trial, resulting in higher MCAVmean%baseline. Indeed, cerebral blood flow can increase in response to elevations in arterial pressure (46). Although the reason behind the sustained higher MCAVmean%baseline during the latter stage of exercise in the Ice trial remains unclear, decreases in core temperature achieved with ice slurry may alleviate hyperthermia-induced increases in sympathetic nerve activity (47), which may contribute to cerebral vasoconstriction (48,49), thereby increasing MCAVmean%baseline. Alternatively, because parasympathetic pathways may mediate cerebral vasodilation in humans (49), ice slurry ingestion may increase parasympathetic activity to cerebral artery, mediating cerebral vasodilation. In addition, reductions in V˙E may have increased blood flow distribution to the brain instead of respiratory muscles, considering that hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation increases work of breathing (14). Future studies are warranted to elucidate the underlying mechanism(s) for the increases in MCAVmean%baseline associated with ice slurry.

sec.title: High-intensity exercise following moderate-intensity exercise
sec.p: During the high-intensity exercise, there were no significant differences in exercise duration between trials (Fig. 5A). However, 8 of the 10 participants exercised for a longer duration in the Ice than the Con trial, with a moderate effect size (r = 0.47). Therefore, although individual variation exists, pre-exercise ice slurry ingestion may enhance late-stage high-intensity endurance performance in the heat. This improvement could be due to increased cerebral blood flow, as assessed by MCAVmean%baseline during moderate-intensity exercise, which may enhance cerebral heat removal and thus maintain lower brain temperature in the Ice condition, delaying central fatigue. On the other hand, V˙O2 during high-intensity exercise was higher in the Ice compared with Con trials (Table 3), suggesting greater oxygen utilization in the active leg muscles. If this is the case, it may attenuate anaerobic lactate production and consequently hydrogen ion production, thereby delaying peripheral fatigue. Further research is warranted to explore the mechanisms by which ice slurry ingestion increases exercise V˙O2 and whether it mitigates central and/or peripheral fatigue.

Regarding individual variations in exercise performance, one participant was identified as an outlier for Δexercise duration, based on the z-score and the Grubbs test. Notably, this same participant was also identified as an outlier for Δ V˙E, ΔPETCO2, Δstomachache, and Δfeeling of diarrhea during moderate-intensity exercise (i.e., before high-intensity exercise). It seems therefore that the extreme increases in ventilation and/or gastrointestinal complaints induced by ice slurry ingestion negated the benefits of ice slurry ingestion and impair subsequent high-intensity exercise performance in this participant. Indeed, gastrointestinal complications associated with any food or fluid intake appear to impair endurance performance (50). Therefore, it appears that ice slurry can exert both positive and negative effects, and if the latter outweighs the former, performance may be compromised. Although the negative effects of interventions are often less emphasized, future research in this area is practically important to maximize the benefits of ice slurry ingestion while minimizing its detrimental influences, thereby further optimizing exercise performance in the heat.

sec.title: Considerations
sec.p: There are several considerations to note in this study. First, we are uncertain if the pill remained in the stomach throughout the experiment or accurately reflected the stomach temperature. Second, the magnitude of the decreases in ingestible temperature associated with ice slurry diminished over the course of exercise (Fig. 2C), which may explain in part the relatively minor contribution of stomach temperature in the regulation of hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation. Third, ice slurry ingestion directly cools the probe used for esophageal temperature measurement. However, we visually confirmed a recovery process for esophageal temperature after the last ice slurry ingestion and a plateau before exercise. Fourth, we used PETCO2 as a surrogate of arterial CO2 partial pressure; the actual changes in arterial CO2 partial pressure might have been different. Fifth, we indirectly assessed cerebral blood flow by measuring MCAVmean using a transcranial Doppler device. This method may not reflect actual changes in cerebral blood flow, as a transcranial Doppler device only measures cerebral blood velocity, whereas the diameter of middle cerebral artery may change in response to alterations in arterial CO2 partial pressure or blood pressure (23,51,52). Lastly, our results can be generalizable to only young males. However, because the sensitivity of hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation during moderate-intensity exercise in females (53) is comparable to that observed in the control trial of the present study, our results pertaining to hyperthermia-induced hyperventilation might be applicable to young females. Nevertheless, further study is warranted to assess this possibility. In addition, it remains to be determined if our findings on MCAVmean responses are applicable to young females, given the reported sex-related differences in cerebrovascular CO2 reactivity (54,55).

sec.title: CONCLUSIONS
sec.p: During prolonged exercise in the heat, pre-exercise ice slurry ingestion reduces exercise ventilation primarily due to decreases in core rather than gastrointestinal temperature, and increases middle cerebral artery mean blood velocity. However, the enhancement of endurance performance in the heat with pre-exercise ice slurry ingestion may depend on the presence of accompanying adverse effects.

ack.p: This study was supported by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology in Japan (grant number: 22H00499). Data will be made available upon reasonable request. We sincerely thank all volunteer participants in this study. We also thank Yin-Feng Lai and Jumpei Kojima for assisting with the data collection. The results of this study are presented clearly, honestly, and without fabrication, falsification, or inappropriate data manipulation. The results of this study do not constitute endorsement by the American College of Sports Medicine.

fn.p: Supplemental digital content is available for this article. Direct URL citations appear in the printed text and are provided in the HTML and PDF versions of this article on the journal’s Web site (www.acsm-msse.org).
